options.text: Add 'only report high-confidence findings' to the existing instructions so the model filters uncertain results
Add a post-processing step that randomly samples 50% of flags to reduce alert volume
Temporarily disable all security alerts until the model can be retrained on the team's codebase
Replace the vague instruction with explicit criteria: 'flag only when the claimed behavior directly contradicts the actual code logic'

answers.explanation: Explicit criteria that describe exactly what constitutes a valid finding are the most effective way to reduce false positives. 'Be thorough' is ambiguous — it tells the model to flag broadly. Concrete criteria like 'flag only when claimed behavior contradicts actual code' provide a precise decision boundary. 'Only report high-confidence findings' fails because model self-confidence is poorly calibrated; it doesn't improve precision.

answers.question: A developer is working on a long refactoring task in a named session. Midway through, the CI pipeline updates several auto-generated configuration files. The developer resumes the session an hour later without informing the agent. What is the most likely consequence?

options.text: The agent will automatically re-read all files in the repository to detect changes.
The agent may make decisions based on the pre-change file content, introducing bugs or reverting the auto-generated updates.
The session will fail to resume because the file checksums no longer match the session state.
The agent will prompt the user to list all changed files before proceeding.

answers.explanation: The agent's in-context model of the codebase includes the pre-change state of those configuration files. If not informed of the updates, it may write code that references stale configuration values, or worse, overwrite the auto-generated updates with a version it constructs from its stale understanding. Explicitly informing the agent about changes is the developer's responsibility.

answers.question: A developer asks Claude to fix a single, well-scoped typo in a configuration variable name that appears in exactly one file. Which execution mode is appropriate?

options.text: Plan mode — all code changes should be planned before execution to ensure safety
Direct execution — the task is simple, well-scoped, and affects a single file
Plan mode — any change to configuration requires architectural review
Direct execution, but only after running /memory to ensure no conflicting conventions

answers.explanation: Direct execution is appropriate for simple, well-scoped, single-file changes with clear scope. A typo fix in one configuration file meets all these criteria. Plan mode is reserved for complex tasks, large-scale changes, architectural decisions, and multi-file modifications — not every change requires it.

answers.question: A tool description reads: 'search_knowledge_base — searches the knowledge base.' An engineer reports the agent never uses this tool even when users ask questions that could be answered by it. What addition to the description would most improve selection reliability?

options.text: A longer list of synonyms for 'knowledge base,' such as 'documentation, wiki, help articles, FAQs.'
Example queries this tool handles (e.g., 'how do I reset my password?'), the input format it expects, edge cases like partial matches, and when to prefer it over web_search.
A note that the tool is optional and should only be used when the model has high confidence the answer exists.
The estimated latency of the tool so the model can decide whether calling it is worth the time.

answers.explanation: An effective tool description should include: what type of queries the tool handles (with examples), the expected input format, edge case behaviors, and when to prefer this tool over alternatives. Synonyms (A) help keyword matching but don't clarify usage. Making the tool 'optional' (C) reduces selection likelihood. Latency (D) is not a selection criterion the model uses.

answers.question: A coordinator is given the task 'Research the economic impact of renewable energy adoption.' It decomposes this into 47 highly specific subtasks, each assigned to a separate subagent. What architectural risk does this create?

options.text: The coordinator will run out of available tool slots because Claude only supports up to 20 simultaneous tool definitions.
Overly narrow decomposition may leave coverage gaps, with no subagent responsible for cross-cutting themes like policy interaction or regional variation.
Parallel execution of 47 subagents will cause API rate limiting that degrades performance.
Each subagent will independently re-research the same foundational background, wasting compute.

answers.explanation: Overly granular task decomposition is a known anti-pattern. When tasks are sliced too narrowly, important cross-cutting themes fall between the cracks — no single subagent is responsible for synthesizing how different domains interact. The coordinator must balance specificity (enabling focused subagent work) with breadth (ensuring full topic coverage).

answers.question: Which of the following is an example of a programmatic prerequisite gate as opposed to a prompt-based guideline?

options.text: A system prompt instruction: 'Always verify the user's identity before accessing account data.'
A few-shot example showing the agent checking credentials before accessing data.
A middleware function that intercepts any call to get_account_data and returns an error if no valid session token is present.
A model fine-tuned to prioritize identity verification in its response generation.

answers.explanation: A programmatic prerequisite is code-level enforcement that executes regardless of model behavior. Option C is a middleware gate — it operates outside the model and provides a deterministic guarantee. Options A and B are probabilistic prompt-based guidance. Option D shifts the probability distribution but still cannot guarantee compliance.

answers.question: Your project has a top-level CLAUDE.md with general guidelines. The backend/ subdirectory has unique database migration rules that should only apply when Claude works in that directory. What is the correct approach?

options.text: Add a CLAUDE.md file inside the backend/ directory with the migration-specific rules
Add a conditional section to the root CLAUDE.md with an 'if directory == backend/' prefix
Create a .claude/rules/ file that imports the backend rules using @import backend/rules.md
Add backend-specific rules to the user-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md with a path filter

answers.explanation: Claude Code supports directory-level CLAUDE.md files. A CLAUDE.md placed inside backend/ is loaded when Claude works on files in that directory, applying those rules contextually. Root CLAUDE.md does not support conditional sections, and .claude/rules/ files use glob patterns to match file paths rather than directory context.

answers.question: Your extraction system fails semantic validation on 15% of invoices where the stated total doesn't match the sum of extracted line items. You want to use this information in your retry logic. What approach most effectively targets this specific error?

options.text: Append the discrepancy ('stated_total: $1,240 but line items sum to $1,180') as specific feedback in the retry prompt
Retry with a generic instruction to 'double-check all numbers' without specifying which validation failed
Bypass validation for semantic errors and only retry on schema syntax errors
Add the invoice to a manual review queue and skip automated retry for semantic errors

answers.explanation: Providing specific discrepancy details in the retry prompt allows Claude to understand exactly what's inconsistent and re-examine those specific values. Generic instructions like 'double-check numbers' are vague and less effective. Semantic errors are worth retrying since the information exists in the document and Claude may have misread a digit; only absent information is irretrievable.

answers.question: You've written detailed instructions for a classification task but the agent still misclassifies edge cases at the boundary between two categories. Despite revising the instructions three times, performance on these boundary cases hasn't improved. What should you try next?

options.text: Accept that boundary cases cannot be reliably classified and route them all to manual review
Add more detailed prose descriptions of both categories to eliminate all remaining ambiguity
Add 2-4 few-shot examples specifically demonstrating the ambiguous boundary cases with reasoning for each classification decision
Increase the instruction length until the model has enough context to handle edge cases

answers.explanation: Few-shot examples are most effective for consistently formatted, actionable output when detailed instructions alone produce inconsistency. Boundary cases are precisely the scenario where showing WHY one classification was chosen over another is more effective than describing the categories further in prose. More prose description of the same concepts rarely helps when descriptions have already been revised multiple times.

answers.question: You want to systematically analyze which types of code constructs are triggering false-positive security findings. What field should you include in your structured output schema?

options.text: A 'false_positive_probability' float field so you can filter findings below a confidence threshold
A 'detected_pattern' field that captures which code construct triggered the finding, enabling systematic false-positive analysis
A 'category_rationale' text field explaining why the finding was categorized as it was
A 'similar_findings_count' integer field tracking how often the same issue has been flagged previously

answers.explanation: The 'detected_pattern' field captures which specific code construct triggered each finding. Aggregating this field across false-positive alerts enables systematic dismissal analysis — identifying patterns (e.g., 'string concatenation in logging statements' always false-positive for SQL injection) that can then be explicitly excluded from the detection criteria.

answers.question: A developer wants a custom /standup slash command that generates a daily standup summary but it should only be available to them personally and should not appear when teammates use the project. Where should this command be placed?

options.text: .claude/commands/ in the project repository
~/.claude/commands/ in their home directory
A commands: section in the project's CLAUDE.md
.claude/skills/ with a personal: true flag in the frontmatter

answers.explanation: User-scoped custom slash commands belong in ~/.claude/commands/ in the developer's home directory. These are personal and not shared via version control. Project-scoped commands in .claude/commands/ are version-controlled and available to all project contributors.

answers.question: You're using plan mode to design a database schema migration. After Claude proposes the plan, you realize one aspect needs a different approach. What is the key advantage of plan mode at this point?

options.text: Plan mode automatically creates a git branch for the changes so you can review a diff before merging
Claude runs all planned changes in a sandbox environment and shows you test results before applying them
You can approve individual steps of the plan while Claude executes others in parallel
You can safely refine the approach before any changes are committed, exploring alternatives without side effects

answers.explanation: Plan mode enables safe exploration before committing — you can review, refine, and course-correct the approach without any actual changes having been made. This is its primary value for complex tasks. Claude Code does not automatically create git branches, run sandboxed executions, or parallelize plan steps.

answers.question: You're building an agent that categorizes customer support tickets. You have 3 clear categories and a few ambiguous edge cases that always cause mistakes. How many few-shot examples should you include and what should they focus on?

options.text: Include 2-4 targeted examples focusing on the ambiguous scenarios, showing the reasoning for why each was categorized a particular way
Include 10+ examples covering all possible category combinations to ensure comprehensive coverage
Include 1 example per category showing a typical case, since examples should cover the common case not the edge case
Avoid few-shot examples and rely on detailed category descriptions to prevent the model from overfitting to example patterns

answers.explanation: The optimal few-shot prompting strategy is 2-4 targeted examples focusing on ambiguous scenarios where instructions alone fail, showing the reasoning behind each decision. Flooding the prompt with 10+ examples increases token cost without proportional benefit. Including only typical cases fails to address the known problem cases. Few-shot examples don't cause overfitting — they enable generalization to novel patterns.

answers.question: Your agent encounters a case requiring a 15% exception to the standard return policy. No policy document covers this scenario. How should the agent respond?

options.text: Apply the closest applicable policy and document the exception in the case notes
Deny the exception and explain the standard return policy to the customer
Escalate to a human agent because policy gaps — not just complex cases — are a defined escalation trigger
Ask Claude to infer what the policy would likely say based on similar policy documents

answers.explanation: Policy gaps are a defined escalation trigger — when an agent encounters a situation not covered by its policies, escalation is appropriate regardless of whether the case appears 'complex' by other measures. Applying the closest policy without authorization risks committing the company to unintended outcomes. Denying without authority is presumptuous. Inferring policy from similar documents could be wrong.

answers.question: A developer is designing a subagent that performs database queries. The subagent will be spawned by a coordinator and must return results in a format the coordinator can use for further delegation. Which data format choice best preserves attribution and supports downstream processing?

options.text: Return results as a single concatenated prose paragraph summarizing the findings.
Return results as a structured JSON array with fields for each record, including source table, query executed, and retrieval timestamp.
Return only the count of matching records to minimize token usage.
Return a SQL dump of the matching rows in raw format without additional metadata.

answers.explanation: Structured data formats (like JSON with explicit fields) preserve attribution — the coordinator and downstream subagents can trace each piece of information to its source, understand when it was retrieved, and determine its provenance. Prose loses structure. Counts lose content. Raw SQL dumps lack the metadata needed for intelligent downstream processing.

answers.question: An agent is given 18 tools covering every possible action in a CRM system, even though most interactions only require 3-5 of them. Users report the agent frequently takes wrong actions. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The agent's context window is exceeded by the tool schema definitions, causing truncation.
Having too many tools degrades selection reliability — the model must distinguish among 18 options and is more likely to select a semantically similar but incorrect tool.
The CRM system's API rate limits are being exceeded because the agent calls too many different tools per session.
The model's attention mechanism cannot process more than 10 tool schemas simultaneously.

answers.explanation: Providing too many tools degrades tool selection reliability. When an agent has 18 tools, many of which are semantically similar (update_account, modify_account, patch_account_record), the model's likelihood of selecting the correct one decreases. Scoping tool access to only the 4-5 tools relevant to an agent's role dramatically improves selection accuracy.

answers.question: A Tier 2 support agent needs to escalate a complex billing dispute to a human specialist. What should the handoff summary include to be maximally useful?

options.text: The full raw transcript of the conversation so the specialist can read everything in context.
The customer's account ID, root cause of the dispute, steps already taken, and the recommended action for the specialist.
A brief one-line description of the issue category, e.g., 'billing dispute.'
The agent's confidence score for each possible resolution path.

answers.explanation: A structured handoff summary should give the human specialist exactly what they need to act: the customer identifier, what the root cause is, what the agent already tried, and what action is recommended. A full raw transcript (A) is overwhelming and unstructured. A one-line description (C) lacks actionable detail. Confidence scores (D) are not what a specialist needs to take action.

answers.question: What does fork_session provide that simply opening two new sessions does not?

options.text: Lower API costs due to shared token billing between forked sessions.
A common starting context (shared baseline) that both forked sessions inherit, avoiding redundant context reconstruction.
The ability to merge the two sessions' outputs into a single result automatically.
Real-time synchronization between the two forked sessions as they diverge.

answers.explanation: fork_session creates two sessions that share a validated starting baseline. Each session then diverges independently. The key advantage over two fresh sessions is that neither needs to re-establish the baseline context — the shared starting point is inherited. Two new sessions would require each to independently re-read files, re-execute setup steps, and rebuild understanding from scratch.

answers.question: Your team has run a code review on a 14-file pull request in the same session that generated the code. The reviewer notes: 'This is the same model that wrote the code — it's likely to repeat the same blind spots.' What is the principle being invoked?

options.text: Self-review limitation: a model that wrote code retains the generation reasoning context and is less likely to question its own decisions
Context contamination: the original code generation filled the context window, leaving less capacity for thorough review
Confirmation bias: larger models consistently confirm their own outputs rather than finding errors
Attention dilution: reviewing 14 files causes attention to spread too thin for quality review

answers.explanation: The self-review limitation captures why same-session review is less effective: the model that generated the code retains the reasoning and assumptions from generation, making it less likely to question decisions it already 'committed to.' An independent review instance — without prior context of the generation process — is more likely to catch bugs and question assumptions.

answers.question: What is the primary purpose of the isRetryable boolean field in an MCP structured error response?

options.text: To indicate whether the tool supports idempotent retries without side effects.
To communicate to the calling agent whether attempting the same operation again is likely to succeed, enabling appropriate retry or escalation decisions.
To configure the maximum number of retries the tool will accept before permanently blocking the caller.
To specify whether the error should be logged at the INFO or ERROR level in the agent's audit trail.

answers.explanation: The isRetryable flag is a signal for the calling agent's decision logic: if true, retry with backoff (likely a transient issue); if false, do not retry (the error is deterministic and repeating will not help). This allows agents to handle different error categories appropriately rather than applying a one-size-fits-all retry policy or giving up on all errors.

answers.question: Two tools in a customer support agent — escalate_to_human and create_callback_request — have overlapping descriptions: both mention 'when customer needs human assistance.' The agent randomly picks between them. What is the correct fix?

options.text: Remove one of the tools to eliminate ambiguity.
Rewrite both descriptions to clearly articulate distinct use cases: escalate_to_human for immediate live transfer, create_callback_request for scheduling a future callback.
Add a priority ranking in the system prompt indicating which tool to prefer when both apply.
Combine the two tools into a single human_handoff tool with a 'type' parameter.

answers.explanation: Ambiguous overlapping descriptions cause misrouting. The correct fix is to make each tool's description precise and mutually exclusive in its use case. Describing the exact difference (live transfer vs. scheduled callback) gives the model the information it needs to choose correctly. Removing a tool (A) sacrifices functionality. Priority hints (C) are a workaround that doesn't fix the root cause.

answers.question: You have a tool_use call with tool_choice set to 'auto'. In some responses, the model returns a text explanation instead of calling the tool. You need the model to always use a tool. What should you change?

options.text: Change tool_choice to 'any' to require the model to call at least one tool
Change tool_choice to 'required' to mandate tool usage
Add 'you must always use a tool' to the system prompt alongside tool_choice: 'auto'
Remove tool_choice entirely — without it, the model defaults to always using tools

answers.explanation: tool_choice: 'any' requires the model to call at least one of the provided tools instead of returning a text response. tool_choice: 'auto' allows the model to decide whether to call a tool or respond with text, which is why it sometimes returns text. 'required' is not a valid tool_choice value. Prompt instructions alone are advisory and don't guarantee tool usage.

answers.question: Your CI pipeline invokes Claude Code to run tests and verify code quality, but Claude seems unaware of your project's testing framework, fixture locations, and coding standards each time it runs. What is the most appropriate solution?

options.text: Add a CLAUDE.md file to the project that documents testing standards, fixture paths, and conventions — Claude Code loads this automatically
Pass the testing standards as a long --context flag argument each time the CI command runs
Create a CI-specific wrapper script that prepends context to every Claude Code invocation
Use the --memory flag to point Claude Code to a shared memory file stored in the CI artifact store

answers.explanation: CLAUDE.md is the standard mechanism for providing project context — including testing standards, fixture locations, and coding conventions — to Claude Code. It's version-controlled, automatically loaded when Claude Code runs in the project, and available to all invocations including CI. Passing context as flags on every invocation is fragile and error-prone.

answers.question: You want to systematically analyze which types of code constructs are triggering false-positive security findings. What field should you include in your structured output schema?

options.text: A 'false_positive_probability' float field so you can filter findings below a confidence threshold
A 'detected_pattern' field that captures which code construct triggered the finding, enabling systematic false-positive analysis
A 'category_rationale' text field explaining why the finding was categorized as it was
A 'similar_findings_count' integer field tracking how often the same issue has been flagged previously

answers.explanation: The 'detected_pattern' field captures which specific code construct triggered each finding. Aggregating this field across false-positive alerts enables systematic dismissal analysis — identifying patterns (e.g., 'string concatenation in logging statements' always false-positive for SQL injection) that can then be explicitly excluded from the detection criteria.

answers.question: You're building a complex new feature but you're not entirely sure about all the requirements and edge cases. Before Claude starts implementing, you want to surface unknowns. What iterative refinement pattern is most effective here?

options.text: Write a detailed requirements document and pass it as context before prompting
Use the interview pattern: ask Claude to ask you clarifying questions before implementing
Start implementation immediately and use Claude's questions during implementation to refine
Provide a minimal prompt and iterate on the output until it matches what you intended

answers.explanation: The interview pattern — having Claude ask clarifying questions before implementing — proactively surfaces requirements gaps and ambiguities before work begins. This avoids costly rework from missed requirements. Writing a requirements document is useful but doesn't guarantee all edge cases are surfaced. Starting implementation immediately with unclear requirements is inefficient.

answers.question: You're using tool_use to extract invoice data into a structured format. The schema validation passes consistently, but you notice extracted line-item totals don't sum to the reported invoice total in the document. What kind of error is this and how do you address it?

options.text: This is a schema error — add a custom validator to the JSON schema that enforces the sum constraint
This is a hallucination problem — switch to a larger model with better arithmetic capabilities
This is a syntax error — the schema isn't strict enough; add number format constraints to line item fields
This is a semantic error — strict JSON schemas prevent syntax errors but not semantic inconsistencies; add a 'calculated_total' field and validate it against 'stated_total' post-extraction

answers.explanation: Strict JSON schemas eliminate syntax errors but do not prevent semantic errors — values that are structurally valid but logically inconsistent. The solution is semantic validation: include both a 'calculated_total' (sum of line items) and 'stated_total' (from the document) field, then detect discrepancies programmatically. JSON schema validators cannot enforce cross-field arithmetic constraints.

answers.question: An MCP server for a project management system exposes both tools (create_issue, update_issue) and resources (project_hierarchy, open_issue_summaries). What is the primary benefit of exposing resources?

options.text: Resources allow the agent to modify server-side data without using tool calls.
Resources expose content catalogs (like issue summaries or documentation hierarchies) that the agent can browse to reduce exploratory tool calls needed to understand available data.
Resources are processed faster than tool calls because they bypass the model's reasoning step.
Resources enable the MCP server to push updates to the agent without waiting for a tool call.

answers.explanation: MCP resources expose structured content catalogs — summaries, hierarchies, indexes — that help the agent understand what data is available without making multiple exploratory tool calls. For example, an open_issue_summaries resource lets the agent scan all open issues in one step, rather than making individual lookup_issue calls until it finds what it needs.

answers.question: What is the correct CLI syntax to resume a named Claude Code session called 'feature-auth'?

options.text: `claude --continue feature-auth`
`claude --resume feature-auth`
`claude --session feature-auth --load`
`claude resume --name feature-auth`

answers.explanation: The correct syntax is `--resume <session-name>`. This flag instructs Claude Code to load the named session's context and continue from where it left off. The other options use incorrect flag names or command structures that are not part of the Claude Code CLI interface.

answers.question: A research workflow requires: (1) identify sub-topics from a broad question, (2) research each sub-topic in parallel, (3) synthesize findings. Which decomposition pattern fits this structure?

options.text: Pure prompt chaining, because the steps are sequential.
Dynamic decomposition where step 1 determines the plan for steps 2 and 3 based on the identified sub-topics.
A single subagent that handles all three phases sequentially in one context window.
Human-in-the-loop decomposition where a person assigns sub-topics to subagents.

answers.explanation: This is a canonical dynamic decomposition scenario. The output of step 1 (sub-topic identification) is not known in advance, so it cannot be statically pre-configured in a prompt chain. The coordinator dynamically creates step-2 subagent assignments based on the sub-topics discovered in step 1. This adaptive planning is exactly what dynamic decomposition enables.

answers.question: Your document extraction system reports 97% overall accuracy across all document types. The operations team is satisfied. However, you want to verify the system is performing well across all document categories, not just on average. What monitoring approach is needed?

options.text: Increase the overall accuracy target to 99.5% to ensure all subcategories perform well
Review only the extractions flagged as low-confidence by the model, since high-confidence extractions are reliable
The 97% aggregate is sufficient — if overall performance is high, subcategory performance is unlikely to be problematic
Stratified random sampling by document type to measure error rates per category — aggregate accuracy can mask poor performance on specific types

answers.explanation: Aggregate accuracy (97% overall) can mask poor performance on specific document types or fields. A system that performs at 99.9% on common document types but 60% on rare invoice formats has a 97% aggregate but a real production problem. Stratified random sampling by document type reveals these category-specific failure modes. Only reviewing low-confidence extractions misses systematic errors in high-confidence predictions.

answers.question: You want Claude to implement a new data processing function and you know the expected behavior well. Which iterative refinement approach reduces the most back-and-forth while producing the most reliable implementation?

options.text: Write the tests first that define the expected behavior, then have Claude implement the function and iterate using test failures
Describe the function in detail and ask Claude to implement it, then manually review and request corrections
Ask Claude to generate three different implementations and select the best one
Use plan mode to get Claude's implementation proposal, then switch to direct execution

answers.explanation: Test-driven iteration — writing tests first and sharing test failures with Claude — provides machine-verifiable feedback that precisely identifies what's wrong. This is more reliable than manual review (which misses edge cases) and more efficient than generating multiple implementations or using plan mode when the requirements are already clear.

answers.question: You want to create a custom /review slash command that runs your team's standard code review checklist. This command should be available to every developer when they clone or pull the repository. Where should you create this command file?

options.text: In the .claude/commands/ directory in the project repository.
In ~/.claude/commands/ in each developer's home directory.
In the CLAUDE.md file at the project root.
In a .claude/config.json file with a commands array.

answers.explanation: Project-scoped custom slash commands should be stored in the .claude/commands/ directory within the repository. These commands are version-controlled and automatically available to all developers when they clone or pull the repo. Option B (~/.claude/commands/) is for personal commands not shared via version control. Option C (CLAUDE.md) is for project instructions and context, not command definitions. Option D describes a configuration mechanism that doesn't exist in Claude Code.

answers.question: When a Claude agent connects to an MCP server, how and when does it discover the available tools?

options.text: The agent discovers tools lazily — only when it attempts to call a tool for the first time does it query the server for its schema.
All tools from the connected MCP server are discovered at connection time and become available simultaneously for the duration of the session.
The agent must explicitly call a list_tools endpoint on each MCP server before any tools are available.
Tools are discovered incrementally as the conversation progresses, with new tools revealed based on conversational context.

answers.explanation: Upon connection to an MCP server, all of its configured tools are discovered and made available simultaneously. The agent has the full tool catalog available from the start of the session, not incrementally or lazily. This is why careful tool curation and scoped access matter — all discovered tools compete for selection from the first turn.

answers.question: An agent is building understanding of an unfamiliar codebase. What is the most efficient tool-use strategy to trace the application's execution flow from entry point to a specific module?

options.text: Read every file in the repository sequentially until the relevant code is found.
Use Grep to find the main entry point, then Read the entry file to identify imports, then Grep for each import to trace the call chain incrementally.
Use Glob to list all files, then Read each one in parallel to build a complete picture before starting analysis.
Use Edit to insert console.log statements at potential entry points and observe which ones execute.

answers.explanation: Building codebase understanding incrementally is the efficient approach: Grep to find entry points, Read to trace imports and call chains, Grep again to follow each import deeper. This targeted exploration follows the actual execution path without reading irrelevant files. Reading everything sequentially (A) or in bulk (C) wastes tokens on files that may be irrelevant.

answers.question: Your support agent calls tools in a loop. After issuing a tool call, what does the stop_reason value 'tool_use' in the API response indicate the agent should do?

options.text: Terminate the loop because the model has finished its reasoning.
Continue the loop by executing the tool and appending the result to conversation history.
Restart the loop from scratch, discarding any previous tool results.
Pause execution and wait for human approval before proceeding.

answers.explanation: A stop_reason of 'tool_use' means the model has requested one or more tool calls and expects the agent to execute them, append the results to conversation history, and continue the loop. The loop only terminates when stop_reason is 'end_turn' (or a similar final state), signaling the model has produced its final answer.

answers.question: After running the multi-agent research system on the topic 'impact of AI on creative industries,' each subagent completes successfully. However, the final reports cover only visual arts, completely missing music, writing, and film production. The coordinator's logs show it decomposed the topic into: 'AI in digital art creation,' 'AI in graphic design,' and 'AI in photography.' What is the most likely root cause?

options.text: The synthesis agent lacks instructions for identifying coverage gaps in the findings it receives from other agents.
The coordinator agent's task decomposition is too narrow, resulting in subagent assignments that don't cover all relevant domains of the topic.
The web search agent's queries are not comprehensive enough and need to be expanded to cover more creative industry sectors.
The document analysis agent is filtering out sources related to non-visual creative industries due to overly restrictive relevance criteria.

answers.explanation: The coordinator's logs reveal the root cause directly: it decomposed 'creative industries' into only visual arts subtasks, completely omitting music, writing, and film. The subagents executed their assigned tasks correctly — the problem is what they were assigned. Options A, C, and D incorrectly blame downstream agents that are working correctly within their assigned scope.

answers.question: Your team wants to reduce API costs for automated analysis. Currently, real-time Claude calls power two workflows: (1) a blocking pre-merge check that must complete before developers can merge, and (2) a technical debt report generated overnight for review the next morning. Your manager proposes switching both to the Message Batches API for its 50% cost savings. How should you evaluate this proposal?

options.text: Use batch processing for the technical debt reports only; keep real-time calls for pre-merge checks.
Switch both workflows to batch processing with status polling to check for completion.
Keep real-time calls for both workflows to avoid batch result ordering issues.
Switch both to batch processing with a timeout fallback to real-time if batches take too long.

answers.explanation: The Message Batches API offers 50% cost savings but has processing times up to 24 hours with no guaranteed latency SLA. This makes it unsuitable for blocking pre-merge checks where developers wait for results, but ideal for overnight batch jobs like technical debt reports. Option B is wrong because relying on batch completion isn't acceptable for blocking workflows. Option C reflects a misconception — batch results can be correlated using custom_id fields. Option D adds unnecessary complexity when the simpler solution is matching each API to its appropriate use case.

answers.question: An engineer argues that model-driven decision-making in agentic loops is inferior to pre-configured decision trees because decision trees are deterministic. Which statement best captures the trade-off?

options.text: Model-driven decision-making is always preferable because it can handle any task without explicit programming.
Pre-configured decision trees are superior in all cases because they are fully auditable.
Decision trees excel at predictable, bounded workflows, while model-driven approaches handle open-ended tasks where the path cannot be fully enumerated in advance.
Decision trees and model-driven approaches are equivalent in capability; the choice is purely a matter of team preference.

answers.explanation: Pre-configured decision trees are excellent when every branch can be anticipated — they are deterministic and auditable. Model-driven decision-making shines when the task space is open-ended, ambiguous, or requires reasoning about novel situations that cannot be pre-enumerated. The best architectures often combine both: use programmatic gates for critical invariants and model judgment for adaptive reasoning.

answers.question: A team wants to explore two competing approaches to refactoring a module, starting from the same validated baseline. Which session management strategy is most appropriate?

options.text: Run both approaches in the same session sequentially, undoing changes between attempts.
Use fork_session to create two independent sessions from the shared baseline, allowing divergent exploration without contamination.
Create two entirely new sessions with no shared context, requiring each to re-establish the baseline independently.
Use --resume on the same session for both approaches, relying on the model to track which approach it is currently working on.

answers.explanation: fork_session is designed precisely for this use case: it creates two sessions that share a common starting point (the validated baseline) but can diverge independently. This avoids the contamination of running both in the same session and the inefficiency of recreating context from scratch for two separate fresh sessions.

answers.question: A developer is deciding between prompt-based guidance and programmatic enforcement for a workflow step that logs all tool calls for compliance auditing. Which approach is more appropriate and why?

options.text: Prompt-based, because instructing the model to narrate its tool usage is sufficient for audit purposes.
Programmatic, because compliance logging must be deterministic and cannot depend on the model choosing to announce its actions.
Prompt-based, because audit logging is a low-stakes operation that doesn't require programmatic enforcement.
Either approach is equally valid for logging since it is a side-effect operation.

answers.explanation: Compliance auditing requires complete, accurate records. If logging depends on the model choosing to describe its actions, there will be gaps and inaccuracies. Programmatic logging intercepts all tool calls at the infrastructure level, ensuring every action is recorded regardless of model behavior. This is exactly the use case for programmatic enforcement over prompt guidance.

answers.question: You're designing a batch processing system that needs Claude to use a tool to look up information, process the result, then call a second tool based on the first result — all within a single batch request. What limitation must you account for?

options.text: The Batch API does not support multi-turn tool calling within a single request — each request is a single turn
The Batch API supports up to 3 tool calls per request but not more
The Batch API requires all tool calls to be specified upfront in the request's tool_use array
The Batch API supports multi-turn tool calling but only with synchronous tool execution

answers.explanation: The Message Batches API does not support multi-turn tool calling within a single request. Each batch request is processed as a single turn — if the model makes a tool call, there's no mechanism for providing tool results and continuing the conversation within the same batch request. Multi-turn agentic workflows with tool calling require the real-time API.

answers.question: Your agent encounters a case requiring a 15% exception to the standard return policy. No policy document covers this scenario. How should the agent respond?

options.text: Apply the closest applicable policy and document the exception in the case notes
Deny the exception and explain the standard return policy to the customer
Escalate to a human agent because policy gaps — not just complex cases — are a defined escalation trigger
Ask Claude to infer what the policy would likely say based on similar policy documents

answers.explanation: Policy gaps are a defined escalation trigger — when an agent encounters a situation not covered by its policies, escalation is appropriate regardless of whether the case appears 'complex' by other measures. Applying the closest policy without authorization risks committing the company to unintended outcomes. Denying without authority is presumptuous. Inferring policy from similar documents could be wrong.

answers.question: You need to add a new authentication middleware that touches the auth module, the API routes, and the session management service. Which mode should you choose and why?

options.text: Direct execution — adding middleware is a routine task with an established pattern
Plan mode — the task involves multiple files and architectural dependencies that should be understood before making changes
Direct execution — the task is clearly specified so no planning is needed
Plan mode — any task touching more than one file requires mandatory planning

answers.explanation: Multi-file modifications involving architectural integration (auth middleware touching auth module, API routes, and session service) are exactly the scenario where plan mode adds value. Understanding the interactions between these systems before making changes prevents errors. Option D is overly prescriptive — plan mode is appropriate based on complexity, not a strict file count rule.

answers.question: An agent is assigned the open-ended task: 'Improve the performance of our e-commerce platform.' What decomposition strategy is best suited to this task?

options.text: Prompt chaining with a fixed 5-step plan: profile, identify bottlenecks, implement caching, optimize queries, test.
Assign the entire task to a single model invocation with the full codebase in context.
Discovery phase to understand the platform → planning phase to prioritize opportunities → execution phase with prioritized subtasks.
Decompose by team ownership: one subagent per engineering team responsible for a service.

answers.explanation: Open-ended improvement tasks require an adaptive approach: first discover the current state (profile, audit, understand the system), then plan based on findings (prioritize by impact), then execute. Skipping discovery with a fixed plan (A) may address the wrong bottlenecks. Team-based decomposition (D) fragments the task artificially and misses system-wide issues.

answers.question: Your agent calls a CRM tool that returns a 40-field customer record, but the agent only needs 5 fields for its current task. Over a long conversation with many tool calls, the agent becomes slow and sometimes incoherent. What is the most likely cause and fix?

options.text: Verbose tool results accumulate and consume tokens disproportionately; trim tool outputs to only the 5 relevant fields before storing them in context
The agent is making redundant tool calls; add caching to return the same result without re-calling the CRM
The 40-field responses exceed the tool result size limit; implement pagination to fetch fields in batches
The CRM response time is causing latency; switch to an async tool call pattern

answers.explanation: Tool results accumulate in the conversation context and consume tokens disproportionately — a 40-field record when only 5 fields are needed means 35 fields of irrelevant data in context for every tool call. Over many interactions this fills the context window with noise. The fix is to trim verbose tool outputs to only relevant fields before they accumulate in context.

answers.question: Your agent has three tools: get_customer, lookup_order, and process_refund. All three have the description: 'Retrieves information.' During testing, the agent calls the wrong tool in 40% of cases. What is the most fundamental cause of this failure?

options.text: The agent's system prompt does not explicitly list the three tools and their order of priority.
Tool descriptions are the primary mechanism LLMs use for tool selection; identical minimal descriptions give the model no basis to differentiate between the tools.
The tools share similar parameter structures, causing the model to confuse their signatures.
The model requires at least five tools before reliable selection is possible; three tools are too few.

answers.explanation: Tool descriptions are the primary signal LLMs use to decide which tool to call. When all three descriptions are identical ('Retrieves information'), the model has no basis to distinguish them and essentially guesses. Fixing the descriptions — with input formats, example queries, and clear boundaries — is the correct first intervention.

answers.question: Your agent loop receives stop_reason 'end_turn' from the API. What is the correct action?

options.text: Execute any remaining queued tool calls, then continue sending requests to the model.
Terminate the loop and return the model's final text response to the caller.
Append an empty tool result to conversation history and call the API again to confirm.
Reset conversation history and restart the task from the beginning.

answers.explanation: The stop_reason 'end_turn' is the model's signal that it has produced its final response and no further tool calls are pending. The correct behavior is to terminate the agentic loop and surface the response to the caller. Continuing to call the API after 'end_turn' wastes tokens and may produce nonsensical follow-up responses.

answers.question: You're using plan mode to design a database schema migration. After Claude proposes the plan, you realize one aspect needs a different approach. What is the key advantage of plan mode at this point?

options.text: Plan mode automatically creates a git branch for the changes so you can review a diff before merging
Claude runs all planned changes in a sandbox environment and shows you test results before applying them
You can approve individual steps of the plan while Claude executes others in parallel
You can safely refine the approach before any changes are committed, exploring alternatives without side effects

answers.explanation: Plan mode enables safe exploration before committing — you can review, refine, and course-correct the approach without any actual changes having been made. This is its primary value for complex tasks. Claude Code does not automatically create git branches, run sandboxed executions, or parallelize plan steps.

answers.question: A product manager proposes using the Batch API for an automated code review that runs on every pull request and blocks merging until complete. An engineer objects. Who is correct and why?

options.text: The engineer is correct — blocking pre-merge checks require timely completion; Batch API has no guaranteed latency SLA and is inappropriate for blocking workflows
The PM is correct — Batch API reduces costs by 50%, and the savings justify implementing status polling to wait for completion
The engineer is correct — Batch API doesn't support code review tasks, only text summarization
The PM is correct — Batch API processing times are typically under 5 minutes, making it viable for pre-merge checks

answers.explanation: The Message Batches API has processing times up to 24 hours with no guaranteed latency SLA. Blocking pre-merge checks where developers wait for results require timely completion — a check that might take hours or days defeats its purpose. The cost savings don't justify blocking developer workflows. The appropriate use is for latency-tolerant workflows like overnight reports.

answers.question: A customer contacts support and, after a lengthy explanation, explicitly says: 'I don't want to deal with a bot anymore. I need to speak to a human agent right now.' How should a well-designed agent respond?

options.text: Immediately escalate to a human agent without attempting to resolve the issue first
Attempt to resolve the customer's underlying issue first; escalate if resolution fails
Offer to resolve the issue one more time and escalate only if the customer repeats the request
Escalate after completing a sentiment analysis pass to verify the request reflects genuine frustration

answers.explanation: When a customer explicitly requests a human agent, the agent should escalate immediately — this is a non-negotiable trigger that takes absolute priority over any other resolution attempt. Attempting to resolve first, requiring a repeated request, or running sentiment analysis before escalating all violate the principle of respecting an explicit customer demand.

answers.question: What is the primary benefit of loading rules only when editing matching files in .claude/rules/, compared to putting all rules in a monolithic CLAUDE.md?

options.text: It reduces irrelevant context and token usage by loading only rules pertinent to the current file
It improves rule priority — .claude/rules/ files always override CLAUDE.md instructions
It enables rules to be encrypted so they're not visible to all team members
It allows rules to be applied retroactively to existing files in the codebase

answers.explanation: Path-specific rules in .claude/rules/ load only when editing files matching the configured glob patterns. This reduces irrelevant context and token usage — a developer editing a React component doesn't need database migration rules loaded. A monolithic CLAUDE.md loads all rules always, consuming context budget regardless of relevance.

answers.question: A developer wants to apply personal coding preferences—such as preferred variable naming conventions—across all projects they work on, without sharing those preferences with teammates. Where should they place their CLAUDE.md configuration?

options.text: .claude/CLAUDE.md at the project root
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md in their home directory
CLAUDE.md at the repository root
A subdirectory CLAUDE.md closest to the files they edit most

answers.explanation: The user-level CLAUDE.md at ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md applies personal preferences across all projects without being committed to version control. Project-level files (.claude/CLAUDE.md or root CLAUDE.md) are shared via version control and apply to everyone who clones the repo. Subdirectory CLAUDE.md files apply only to that directory tree.

answers.question: During a long codebase exploration session, the main agent's responses shift from citing specific class names and functions found earlier to vague references like 'the typical pattern in this codebase.' What is happening and how should you respond?

options.text: The model is appropriately generalizing from specific findings to patterns, indicating successful learning
Context degradation is occurring — key findings from earlier are being pushed out; use scratchpad files to persist specific findings across context boundaries
The session has hit a token limit — start a new session and re-explore the codebase from scratch
The model is uncertain about earlier findings and is hedging — provide more specific prompts to force precise answers

answers.explanation: Context degradation in extended sessions is characterized by the model transitioning from specific references ('class UserAuthService extends BaseAuth') to vague generalizations ('typical patterns'). This indicates earlier specific findings have been pushed out of active attention. Scratchpad files persist key findings (class names, function signatures, architectural observations) outside the conversation context, making them retrievable across context boundaries.

answers.question: A developer wants to test a new experimental MCP server locally without affecting other team members' configurations. Where should this MCP server be configured?

options.text: In .mcp.json at the project root, so it is easily discoverable by other developers.
In ~/.claude.json (user-level configuration), keeping it personal and separate from the team's project configuration.
In a separate .mcp.experimental.json file in the project root.
As an environment variable EXPERIMENTAL_MCP_SERVER_URL, which Claude automatically detects.

answers.explanation: User-level MCP configuration in ~/.claude.json is the correct location for personal or experimental servers. It is not shared with the team (not in version control) and does not affect other developers' environments. Project-level .mcp.json is for shared tooling the whole team uses.

answers.question: You identify two separate bugs in your codebase. Bug A causes incorrect tax calculations. Bug B causes a UI misalignment in the checkout flow. They exist in completely different modules with no shared code. How should you approach fixing these?

options.text: Fix them in separate, sequential iterations — address Bug A fully, then address Bug B
Report both bugs in a single message so Claude can fix them together
Always use plan mode for multiple bug fixes regardless of their relationship
Combine them only if they're in the same language or framework

answers.explanation: Independent issues should be addressed through sequential iteration — one at a time — to keep each change focused, reviewable, and easier to debug if something goes wrong. The guideline is: multiple interacting issues → single message; independent issues → sequential iteration. These bugs are completely independent, so sequential is correct.

answers.question: You use tool_use with a strict JSON schema for document processing. Your schema includes a required 'contract_value' field. When processing documents where no contract value is mentioned, the model fabricates plausible-looking numbers. How should you fix this?

options.text: Make 'contract_value' nullable/optional in the schema so the model can return null when the information is absent
Add instruction 'never fabricate data' to the system prompt to prevent hallucination
Add a post-processing step that cross-references extracted values against source documents
Use a higher-temperature setting to increase output diversity and reduce repetitive fabrication patterns

answers.explanation: Making fields nullable/optional in the schema is the correct structural fix — it gives the model a valid way to express 'this information is not present' rather than forcing it to fabricate a value to satisfy a required field constraint. Prompt instructions against fabrication are advisory. Post-processing adds complexity but doesn't address the root cause. Temperature doesn't affect hallucination behavior.

answers.question: An engineer needs to ensure the agent always calls the validate_input tool as the first step, regardless of user input. Which tool_choice configuration enforces this?

options.text: tool_choice: 'auto' — the model will infer validate_input is needed from the system prompt.
tool_choice: 'any' — the model will choose validate_input since it is the most relevant tool.
tool_choice: { type: 'tool', name: 'validate_input' } — forces the model to call exactly this tool.
tool_choice: 'required' — forces tool use and the model will always select validate_input first.

answers.explanation: Forced tool_choice with { type: 'tool', name: 'validate_input' } guarantees the model calls exactly the specified tool. 'auto' is probabilistic. 'any' guarantees a tool call but allows the model to pick which one — it may not choose validate_input. There is no 'required' mode that both forces tool use and selects a specific tool.

answers.question: Your multi-agent pipeline has 5 subagents. One subagent fails on a non-critical enrichment step. Which anti-pattern should you avoid?

options.text: Terminating the entire workflow on a single non-critical subagent failure when the pipeline could continue with partial results
Allowing the failed subagent to retry before reporting failure to the coordinator
Reporting the failure to the coordinator and letting it decide how to proceed
Continuing the pipeline with the other 4 subagents' results and flagging the missing enrichment data

answers.explanation: Terminating an entire workflow on a single non-critical failure is an anti-pattern — it's disproportionate and destroys valuable work from successful subagents. The correct approach is graceful degradation: propagate the error with context, let the coordinator assess impact, and continue with partial results when the failed step is non-critical. Allowing retries and continuing with remaining results are both appropriate behaviors.

answers.question: An agentic loop implementation logs the model's final text response and checks whether it contains phrases like 'task complete' or 'I have finished' to decide whether to stop. What is the primary risk of this approach?

options.text: It is computationally expensive to scan text on every iteration.
The model may phrase completion differently, causing the loop to continue unnecessarily or exit prematurely.
It requires storing the full conversation history, which exceeds context limits.
It prevents the model from using tool calls because tool results do not contain text.

answers.explanation: Parsing natural language to detect completion is an anti-pattern because the model's phrasing is non-deterministic. It may say 'Done!' instead of 'task complete,' causing the loop to continue, or phrase an intermediate step as if it were a conclusion, causing premature termination. The correct signal is the structured stop_reason field returned by the API.

answers.question: A developer is designing a subagent that performs database queries. The subagent will be spawned by a coordinator and must return results in a format the coordinator can use for further delegation. Which data format choice best preserves attribution and supports downstream processing?

options.text: Return results as a single concatenated prose paragraph summarizing the findings.
Return results as a structured JSON array with fields for each record, including source table, query executed, and retrieval timestamp.
Return only the count of matching records to minimize token usage.
Return a SQL dump of the matching rows in raw format without additional metadata.

answers.explanation: Structured data formats (like JSON with explicit fields) preserve attribution — the coordinator and downstream subagents can trace each piece of information to its source, understand when it was retrieved, and determine its provenance. Prose loses structure. Counts lose content. Raw SQL dumps lack the metadata needed for intelligent downstream processing.

answers.question: An agent uses Edit to replace a variable name that appears 47 times across a large file. The Edit call fails with 'non-unique match.' What is the correct fallback approach?

options.text: Use Grep to identify all occurrences and then use Edit with a larger surrounding context block for each one.
Use Read to get the full file content, perform the replacement programmatically, and then use Write to overwrite the file.
Use Edit with the replace_all parameter set to true to replace all occurrences simultaneously.
Use the Bash tool with sed to perform a global find-and-replace on the file.

answers.explanation: Edit supports a replace_all parameter designed exactly for this case — replacing all occurrences of a string across a file. When a single targeted replacement fails because the match is not unique, replace_all is the correct tool-native solution. Read + Write is a valid fallback but replace_all is more precise and only changes the intended string.

answers.question: An agent is instructed in its system prompt to always confirm the customer's account status before issuing a refund. In testing, the agent follows this correctly 97% of the time. Should this prompt-based approach be used for production refund processing?

options.text: Yes — 97% compliance is statistically excellent and acceptable for financial operations.
Yes — the remaining 3% will be caught by downstream payment processor validation.
No — prompt instructions have a non-zero failure rate; critical financial sequences require programmatic enforcement to provide deterministic guarantees.
No — prompt-based instructions are never reliable and should never be used for any operational guidance.

answers.explanation: A 3% failure rate translates to significant errors at scale. Prompt instructions alone have a non-zero failure rate that cannot be driven to zero through better prompting. Critical sequences — especially those with financial consequences — require programmatic enforcement (hooks, prerequisite checks) that guarantee the sequence is followed regardless of model behavior.

answers.question: A coordinator is given the task 'Research the economic impact of renewable energy adoption.' It decomposes this into 47 highly specific subtasks, each assigned to a separate subagent. What architectural risk does this create?

options.text: The coordinator will run out of available tool slots because Claude only supports up to 20 simultaneous tool definitions.
Overly narrow decomposition may leave coverage gaps, with no subagent responsible for cross-cutting themes like policy interaction or regional variation.
Parallel execution of 47 subagents will cause API rate limiting that degrades performance.
Each subagent will independently re-research the same foundational background, wasting compute.

answers.explanation: Overly granular task decomposition is a known anti-pattern. When tasks are sliced too narrowly, important cross-cutting themes fall between the cracks — no single subagent is responsible for synthesizing how different domains interact. The coordinator must balance specificity (enabling focused subagent work) with breadth (ensuring full topic coverage).

answers.question: Your validation pipeline rejects Claude's output with a specific error: 'required field phone_number missing.' You want to use retry logic to self-correct. How should you structure the retry prompt?

options.text: Append the specific validation error to the original prompt and ask Claude to regenerate with the error fixed
Send an identical prompt again, since the error may have been a transient generation issue
Ask Claude to reflect on its previous output and identify any missing fields before regenerating
Raise temperature on the retry to increase the chance of a different output that passes validation

answers.explanation: Retry-with-error-feedback — appending specific validation errors to the prompt — is the most effective self-correction strategy. Claude can directly see what went wrong and fix it. Sending the same prompt again provides no new information and produces the same error. Asking Claude to self-reflect without giving the specific error is less efficient. Raising temperature increases randomness but doesn't target the specific missing field.

answers.question: Your multi-agent pipeline has a data retrieval subagent that fails due to a database timeout. Instead of propagating the error, the subagent returns an empty results array, which the coordinator interprets as 'no data found.' What is the problem with this design?

options.text: Access failures (timeout) must be distinguished from valid empty results — silently returning empty as success hides the failure from the coordinator
The subagent should retry indefinitely until it succeeds rather than returning any result
The coordinator should always assume empty results indicate a failure and request manual verification
Database timeouts should terminate the entire pipeline immediately to prevent data inconsistency

answers.explanation: Access failures (timeout, connection error) and valid empty results (successful query with no matching records) are semantically different outcomes that must be communicated differently. Silently treating a failure as success is an anti-pattern — the coordinator cannot make intelligent recovery decisions if it doesn't know a failure occurred.

answers.question: Your security scanning agent has three detection categories: SQL injection (very high false positives), hardcoded secrets (low false positives), and insecure deserialization (moderate false positives). High false-positive rates on SQL injection are causing developers to distrust the accurate hardcoded secrets alerts. What should you do?

options.text: Lower the confidence threshold for all categories equally so the model is more conservative overall
Temporarily disable SQL injection detection while refining its criteria, to preserve trust in the accurate categories
Switch to a higher-parameter model that has better calibration across all security categories
Add a disclaimer to all alerts explaining that false positives are expected in automated security analysis

answers.explanation: High false-positive categories undermine developer trust even in accurate categories. The correct approach is to temporarily disable the high-FP category (SQL injection) while improving its prompts, rather than letting it erode confidence in reliable categories like hardcoded secrets. Lowering confidence thresholds for all categories makes other categories worse. Disclaimers reduce actionability without solving the problem.

answers.question: A developer has configured CLAUDE.md files at multiple levels of their project hierarchy and is unsure which memory files are actually being loaded in the current session. What command should they run to verify this?

options.text: /config --show-files
/debug context
claude --list-rules
/memory

answers.explanation: The /memory command in Claude Code displays which memory files (CLAUDE.md files at all levels) are currently loaded in the session. This is the correct way to verify the active configuration hierarchy.

answers.question: Your agent achieves 55% first-contact resolution, well below the 80% target. Logs show it escalates straightforward cases (standard damage replacements with photo evidence) while attempting to autonomously handle complex situations requiring policy exceptions. What's the most effective way to improve escalation calibration?

options.text: Add explicit escalation criteria to your system prompt with few-shot examples demonstrating when to escalate versus resolve autonomously.
Have the agent self-report a confidence score (1-10) before each response and automatically route requests to humans when confidence falls below a threshold.
Deploy a separate classifier model trained on historical tickets to predict which requests need escalation before the main agent begins processing.
Implement sentiment analysis to detect customer frustration levels and automatically escalate when negative sentiment exceeds a threshold.

answers.explanation: Adding explicit escalation criteria with few-shot examples directly addresses the root cause: unclear decision boundaries. This is the proportionate first response before adding infrastructure. Option B fails because LLM self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated — the agent is already incorrectly confident on hard cases. Option C is over-engineered, requiring labeled data and ML infrastructure when prompt optimization hasn't been tried. Option D solves a different problem entirely — sentiment doesn't correlate with case complexity.

answers.question: Your security scanning agent has three detection categories: SQL injection (very high false positives), hardcoded secrets (low false positives), and insecure deserialization (moderate false positives). High false-positive rates on SQL injection are causing developers to distrust the accurate hardcoded secrets alerts. What should you do?

options.text: Lower the confidence threshold for all categories equally so the model is more conservative overall
Temporarily disable SQL injection detection while refining its criteria, to preserve trust in the accurate categories
Switch to a higher-parameter model that has better calibration across all security categories
Add a disclaimer to all alerts explaining that false positives are expected in automated security analysis

answers.explanation: High false-positive categories undermine developer trust even in accurate categories. The correct approach is to temporarily disable the high-FP category (SQL injection) while improving its prompts, rather than letting it erode confidence in reliable categories like hardcoded secrets. Lowering confidence thresholds for all categories makes other categories worse. Disclaimers reduce actionability without solving the problem.

answers.question: A coordinator is given the task 'Research the economic impact of renewable energy adoption.' It decomposes this into 47 highly specific subtasks, each assigned to a separate subagent. What architectural risk does this create?

options.text: The coordinator will run out of available tool slots because Claude only supports up to 20 simultaneous tool definitions.
Overly narrow decomposition may leave coverage gaps, with no subagent responsible for cross-cutting themes like policy interaction or regional variation.
Parallel execution of 47 subagents will cause API rate limiting that degrades performance.
Each subagent will independently re-research the same foundational background, wasting compute.

answers.explanation: Overly granular task decomposition is a known anti-pattern. When tasks are sliced too narrowly, important cross-cutting themes fall between the cracks — no single subagent is responsible for synthesizing how different domains interact. The coordinator must balance specificity (enabling focused subagent work) with breadth (ensuring full topic coverage).

answers.question: A payment gateway tool times out intermittently under high load. The MCP error response for this scenario should have which configuration?

options.text: isError: true, errorCategory: 'transient', isRetryable: true — so the agent knows it can retry with exponential backoff.
isError: true, errorCategory: 'business', isRetryable: false — to prevent duplicate payment attempts.
isError: false — timeouts should be represented as empty results to avoid triggering error handling.
isError: true, errorCategory: 'validation' — because the request did not complete successfully.

answers.explanation: Transient errors (timeouts, temporary service unavailability) are retryable — the operation may succeed if attempted again after a delay. Marking them as transient with isRetryable: true allows the agent to apply exponential backoff and retry appropriately. Business errors (B) are for policy violations that will never succeed regardless of how many retries are attempted.

answers.question: A coordinator needs to launch three research subagents (web search, document analysis, and database query) simultaneously to minimize latency. How should the coordinator structure its response?

options.text: Issue three separate API responses in sequence, one Task tool call per response, waiting for each to complete before issuing the next.
Emit all three Task tool calls in a single coordinator response so they can be executed in parallel.
Include the three subtasks in a single Task tool call as a JSON array of task descriptions.
Spawn a meta-coordinator subagent and have it issue the three Task calls on the coordinator's behalf.

answers.explanation: To achieve parallel subagent execution, the coordinator must emit multiple Task tool calls in a single response. When the runtime sees multiple tool calls in one response, it can execute them concurrently. Issuing them sequentially in separate API responses forces serial execution, negating the latency benefit of parallelism.

answers.question: Your organization maintains shared coding standards that multiple project repositories should all follow. You want to centralize these standards in one place so updates propagate without editing each project's CLAUDE.md. What is the best approach?

options.text: Store the standards in the user-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md on each developer's machine
Copy and paste the standards block into every project's CLAUDE.md and update them separately
Create a .claude/standards/ directory and Claude will auto-discover files there
Host the standards in a shared file and use @import in each project's CLAUDE.md to reference it

answers.explanation: @import allows CLAUDE.md files to reference external files, enabling a centralized standards file to be imported by multiple project configurations. Changes to the shared file propagate to all importing projects. User-level CLAUDE.md doesn't work across repos for team sharing. Copy-paste creates divergence. .claude/standards/ auto-discovery doesn't exist.

answers.question: Two tools in a customer support agent — escalate_to_human and create_callback_request — have overlapping descriptions: both mention 'when customer needs human assistance.' The agent randomly picks between them. What is the correct fix?

options.text: Remove one of the tools to eliminate ambiguity.
Rewrite both descriptions to clearly articulate distinct use cases: escalate_to_human for immediate live transfer, create_callback_request for scheduling a future callback.
Add a priority ranking in the system prompt indicating which tool to prefer when both apply.
Combine the two tools into a single human_handoff tool with a 'type' parameter.

answers.explanation: Ambiguous overlapping descriptions cause misrouting. The correct fix is to make each tool's description precise and mutually exclusive in its use case. Describing the exact difference (live transfer vs. scheduled callback) gives the model the information it needs to choose correctly. Removing a tool (A) sacrifices functionality. Priority hints (C) are a workaround that doesn't fix the root cause.

answers.question: While exploring a large codebase in plan mode, Claude generates extensive discovery output from reading dozens of files and mapping dependencies. You're concerned this verbose output is consuming the conversation context. What should you use?

options.text: An explore subagent that isolates the discovery output and returns only a summary to the main context
The /compact command to compress the accumulated discovery output before proceeding
Plan mode's built-in context trimming, which automatically summarizes tool results
Split the exploration into multiple separate Claude sessions and manually consolidate the findings

answers.explanation: The explore subagent pattern isolates verbose discovery output from the main conversation. The subagent performs the exploration independently and returns only a concise summary to the main context, preserving valuable context budget. /compact can help in some scenarios but is a blunt instrument; the explore subagent is the specifically designed solution for this pattern.

answers.question: Your system regularly summarizes conversation history but the summaries have become less useful over time. Upon review, you find the model produces summaries like 'Customer had some issues with a previous order and was given compensation.' Critical specifics are lost. What is the most precise fix?

options.text: Instruct the summarizer to explicitly preserve all numerical values, dates, claim IDs, and amounts verbatim rather than paraphrasing them
Increase the summary length limit so summaries can retain more detail
Switch to a larger model for summarization that has better retention of specific details
Use extractive summarization (copy key sentences) instead of abstractive summarization

answers.explanation: The root cause is the summarizer paraphrasing specifics into vague descriptions. The targeted fix is explicit instruction to preserve numerical values, dates, IDs, and amounts verbatim. This addresses the exact failure mode without the overhead of longer summaries, larger models, or extractive approaches that may include irrelevant sentences.

answers.question: Your project has test files (*.test.ts), migration files (db/migrations/*.sql), and config files (config/*.yaml) that each require different conventions. What is the correct way to configure these rules using .claude/rules/?

options.text: Create a single .claude/rules/all-rules.md file and use regex comments to mark which sections apply to which file types
Create separate rule files in .claude/rules/ each with a YAML frontmatter paths field containing the appropriate glob pattern for each file type
Create subdirectories within .claude/rules/ matching the directory structure of the codebase
Add a rules: array in .claude/config.json mapping glob patterns to rule file names

answers.explanation: Each rule file in .claude/rules/ has a YAML frontmatter paths field containing glob patterns. Multiple separate rule files can each target different glob patterns — one for *.test.ts, one for db/migrations/*.sql, etc. Rules load only when editing matching files, reducing irrelevant context. The other options describe mechanisms that don't exist.

answers.question: A PostToolUse hook is used to inject structured metadata (timestamps, request IDs, upstream service version) into every tool result before the model processes it. What is the primary benefit of this pattern?

options.text: It reduces token usage by compressing the tool result before sending it to the model.
It enriches tool results with provenance data, enabling the model to reason about data freshness and source without requiring additional tool calls.
